senses.gloss: post, mail, an institution to send messages, intelligence
messenger, courier, express, estafette, a person employed in the institution of mail or intelligence
the beast by which messages are sent by the mail institution, a post-horse, post-mule or post-camel

examples.text:
عَلى كلّ مَقصُوصِ الذُّنَابى مُعاوِدٍ
 بَرِيدِ السُّرَى باللّيلِ من خيلِ بَرْبرَا

senses.gloss: a measure of distance, a postal route

examples.text: فأمّا الْبَرِيد ففيه خلاف وذهب قوم إلى أنه بالبادية اثنا عشر ميلا وبالشام وخراسان ستة أميال
examples.english: About the postal route there is disagreement. Folks believed that in the desert it is twelve miles and in Syria and Khorasan six miles.

senses.gloss: crumb, piece of bread eaten to a stew for volume

examples.text: وقولون البَرِيدُ لخبز يلقى عليه الماء تَطْعَمُةُ النِّسَاءُ لِلسُّمْنَةِ. والصّوَاب الْمَبْرُودُ.
examples.english: They call barīd the bread thrown into the water the women eat for volume. The right is mabrūd.

etymology.text:
Already in the 6th century, from Byzantine Greek βέρηδος (bérēdos), βέρεδος (béredos, “post-horse”), from Latin verēdus.

Apparently from بَرَدَ (barada, “to cut off”).

body.text: public building for the accommodation of travellers and their beasts,
the like specially appropriated to messengers and the beasts that carry them:
afterwards, it was applied also to
any beast appointed for the conveyance of messengers

A messenger borne on a post-mule

messengers on beasts of the post:
a messenger that journeys with haste:
(S, Msb, K:) pl. as above. (M, * Z.) Hence the saying,
Fever is the messenger of death:
(T, Msb:) because it gives warning thereof. (T.) Hence also

: (T:) the pl. is as above. (T, Z.) A journey of four

so in the following verse of Muzarrid, in praise of 'Arábeh El-Owsee:

fadatoka EaraAba Alyawoma A^um~iY wa xaAlatiY
                      
                      
                        wa naAqatiYa Aln~aAjiY A_ilayoka bariyduhaA
May my mother, and my maternal aunt, and my she-camel that is swift in her course to thee from one station to another, be ransoms for thee, O 'Arábeh,
